content.thinking:
The issue is clear: `es-cluster-2` is failing because there's a null-valued `node.name` setting in its Elasticsearch configuration. The error is:

```
ElasticsearchParseException[null-valued setting found for key [node.name] found at line number [2], column number [11]]
```

This is happening in the ConfigMap `es-config`. Let me check what's in that ConfigMap to understand the problem.

content.text: The issue is clear: `es-cluster-2` fails with a null `node.name` in the elasticsearch.yml config. Let me examine the ConfigMap.

input.command: kubectl get configmap es-config -n elasticsearch -o yaml

content.thinking:
Found the problem. The ConfigMap has `node.name: ` (empty/null value) which causes Elasticsearch to fail with the parse error. 

Also notice the `discovery.seed_hosts` is pointing to the wrong namespace - it's using `default.svc.cluster.local` instead of `elasticsearch.svc.cluster.local`. This could prevent nodes from joining the cluster even after fixing the node.name issue.

Let me check if nodes 0 and 1 are working despite this. They must be using the wrong seed hosts too...

Actually wait - nodes 0 and 1 are running fine. Why? Let me think...
The seed_hosts reference points to `default` namespace but the service is actually in the `elasticsearch` namespace, so the DNS lookup should fail. Yet nodes 0 and 1 already formed a cluster somehow - maybe they discovered each other before the namespace mismatch became an issue, or there's something else allowing the discovery to work despite the incorrect reference.

The real blocker is the null `node.name` in the ConfigMap. I can fix this by using Elasticsearch's environment variable substitution with `${POD_NAME}`, which the pods already have set via the downward API pointing to their actual pod names like `es-cluster-2`.

But I should also verify the discovery.seed_hosts configuration—if it's pointing to the wrong namespace, nodes won't discover each other properly. Let me check the cluster status from one of the running nodes to see if they're already communicating.

content.text: The ConfigMap has `node.name: ` (empty/null) which causes the parse error. Also the `discovery.seed_hosts` reference `default.svc.cluster.local` instead of `elasticsearch.svc.cluster.local`. Let me check the cluster health first.

input.description: Check Elasticsearch cluster health from node 0
Check current cluster nodes from node 0

content.thinking:
Confirmed - the cluster has only 2 nodes (es-cluster-0 and es-cluster-1), cluster status is yellow (1 unassigned shard), and es-cluster-2 is failing to start due to the null `node.name`.

Now I need to fix the ConfigMap:
1. Set `node.name: ${POD_NAME}` to use the pod name environment variable
2. Fix the `discovery.seed_hosts` to use the correct namespace `elasticsearch` instead of `default`

Let me patch the ConfigMap.

content.text:
Two bugs in the ConfigMap:
1. `node.name:` is null — fix to `${POD_NAME}` (the env var is already injected)
2. `discovery.seed_hosts` uses wrong namespace `default` instead of `elasticsearch`

input.command:
kubectl patch configmap es-config -n elasticsearch --type merge -p '{
  "data": {
    "elasticsearch.yml": "cluster.name: es-cluster
node.name: ${POD_NAME}
node.roles: [ master, data, ingest ]
network.host: 0.0.0.0
discovery.seed_hosts:
  - es-cluster-0.es-cluster.elasticsearch.svc.cluster.local
  - es-cluster-1.es-cluster.elasticsearch.svc.cluster.local
  - es-cluster-2.es-cluster.elasticsearch.svc.cluster.local
cluster.initial_master_nodes:
  - es-cluster-0
  - es-cluster-1
  - es-cluster-2
node.store.allow_mmap: false
xpack.security.enabled: false
xpack.security.http.ssl.enabled: false
xpack.security.transport.ssl.enabled: false
"
  }
}'
input.description: Fix the ConfigMap: set node.name to POD_NAME env var and fix discovery seed hosts namespace

content.text: Now delete es-cluster-2 so it restarts and picks up the fixed config:

input.description: Delete the failing pod to force restart with fixed config

input.command:
for i in $(seq 1 30); do
  STATUS=$(kubectl get pod es-cluster-2 -n elasticsearch --no-headers 2>/dev/null | awk '{print $2, $3}')
  echo "[$(date +%T)] $STATUS"
  echo "$STATUS" | grep -q "1/1" && echo "Pod is ready!" && break
  sleep 5
done
input.description: Wait for es-cluster-2 to become ready

description: Wait for es-cluster-2 to become ready
